topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard box arrived smelling faintly of old pencil shavings and lavender. Liam stared at it, his jaw tight. Inside, meticulously stacked, were every single assignment from his entire middle school career—tests, essays, rough drafts, even the doodles scribbled in the margins. He remembered asking his mother, weeks ago, for the old yearbooks. She’d simply smiled, a practiced, bright expression, and said, "Why bother? They’re all in here, just in case." A slow, hot flush crept up his neck. He picked up a crumpled history paper, the ink slightly faded, and felt a strange, heavy weight settle in his chest. He didn't look at his mother, who was currently humming in the kitchen, oblivious. He just stood there, the paper trembling slightly in his grip, the silence of the room amplifying the sudden, sharp feeling of being utterly overlooked.

[story 2]

"Seriously, Mom?" Sarah asked, her voice dangerously level as she surveyed the overflowing pile of textbooks and graded papers spread across the dining table. The sheer volume of work, accumulated over years, felt like a deliberate display. She’d been expecting perhaps a few old notes, maybe a forgotten permission slip. Instead, it was an archive of her academic life, curated and stored with alarming precision. A muscle twitched in her jaw. She picked up a particularly detailed chemistry lab report, the crisp edges feeling cold under her fingertips. A tightness constricted her throat. When her mother finally entered, whistling tunelessly, Sarah didn't move. She just held the papers tighter, her gaze fixed on a spot just over her mother’s shoulder, a silent communication of profound disappointment radiating from her stillness.

[story 3]

Mark found the box tucked away in the attic, hidden beneath a stack of seasonal decorations. When he finally lifted the lid, the sight of organized chaos—pages upon pages of his adolescent efforts—hit him like a physical blow. He felt a sudden, hot prickle behind his eyes. He hadn't asked for this. He hadn't even realized she had collected them all. He ran a hand through his hair, the gesture jerky and agitated. He flipped through a collection of poorly written poetry, his breath catching in his chest. He felt a distinct urge to slam the lid shut, to retreat from the overwhelming evidence of her silent stewardship, feeling small and profoundly unseen in the face of this unexpected, exhaustive collection.
